CommissioIl, 
P~O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
•• 
Administration •• 
Executive/legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
(916) 322-5662 
322...5660 
322·5901 
322-6441 
Dana W. Reed 
Dobbs & Nielsen 
Attorneys at Law 
612 South Flower 
Los Angeles, CA 
Dear Mr. Reed: 
October 15, 1981 
Street, Suite 309 
90017 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Bob Stern has passed along your September 25, 1981, request 
for advice to this division to be answered. 
You have asked 
whether a newsletter prepared by Congressman George Miller con-
stitutes a reportable expenditure under the Political Reform 
Act. 
You have indicated that there are other Congressmen who 
may wish to send similar letters, either for or against the 
Peripheral Canal, and would like to know if such letters are 
reportable under the Act. 
Since you have not identified the 
person or persons on whose behalf you are asking this question, 
I am unable to give you any very specific advice on this matter. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329. 
However, I can answer some 
of your questions in very general terms. 
A person who sends out a newsletter advocating passage or 
defeat of a ballot measure is required to file campaign state-
ments under the Political Reform Act only if the person (or 
persons) constitutes a committee as defined in Section 82013 of 
the Government Code, or is one of the person or persons listed 
in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8225(b}. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8225(c} helps provide a defini-
tion of when a person or persons becomes a committee under Section 
820l3(b} of the Government Code. 
A person or persons who makes 
expenditures of $500 or more during a calendar year on newsletters 
which expressly advocate the passage or defeat of a ballot measure, 
as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8225(c), constitutes an 
independent expenditure committee under Government Code Section 
820l3(b}. 
You also asked whether expenditures on a newsletter are 
exempt from the reporting requirements of the Act. 
Congressional 
newsletters are not included among the list of exempt entities 


Dana W. Reed 
October 15, 1981 
Page Two 
in Government Code Section 86300(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18225(c) (4). 
Since it is my understanding that you have not written for 
advice on behalf of Congressman Miller, I will not offer any 
opinion as to Congressman Miller's possible reporting obliga-
tions in connection with the newsletter which you have provided. 
If you want advice on behalf of any other Congressman, or on 
behalf of any other person, with respect to a particular news-
letter, I will be happy to try to assist you. 
In the meantime, 
I hope this letter has answered most of your questions. 
BAM:plh 
Very truly yours, 
Barbara A. Milman 
Chief 
Legal Division 


Fair Pol 
P. O. Box 
Sacramento, CA

sections.question: Whether a newsletter prepared by Congressman George Miller constitutes a reportable expenditure under the Political Reform Act, and whether similar letters from other Congressmen either for or against the Peripheral Canal are reportable under the Act.
sections.conclusion: A person who sends out a newsletter advocating passage or defeat of a ballot measure is required to file campaign statements under the Political Reform Act only if the person (or persons) constitutes a committee as defined in Section 82013 of the Government Code, or is one of the person or persons listed in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(b). A person or persons who makes expenditures of $500 or more during a calendar year on newsletters which expressly advocate the passage or defeat of a ballot measure constitutes an independent expenditure committee under Government Code Section 82013(b). Congressional newsletters are not included among the list of exempt entities in Government Code Section 86300(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(4).
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether newsletters prepared by Congressmen advocating for or against a ballot measure (the Peripheral Canal) constitute reportable expenditures under California's Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Newsletters advocating passage or defeat of a ballot measure are reportable unless the sender is exempt. Persons spending $500+ annually on such newsletters become independent expenditure committees with filing obligations. Congressional newsletters are not exempt from reporting requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is imperfect with some text degradation on second page. No specific opinion given regarding Congressman Miller since requester had not written on his behalf. Letter clarifies that while Congress-related materials lack specific exemptions, reporting obligations depend on whether sender meets committee definition thresholds.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a newsletter prepared by Congressman George Miller constitutes a reportable expenditure under the Political Reform Act, and whether similar letters from other Congressmen either for or against the Peripheral Canal are reportable under the Act.

CONCLUSION: A person who sends out a newsletter advocating passage or defeat of a ballot measure is required to file campaign statements under the Political Reform Act only if the person (or persons) constitutes a committee as defined in Section 82013 of the Government Code, or is one of the person or persons listed in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(b). A person or persons who makes expenditures of $500 or more during a calendar year on newsletters which expressly advocate the passage or defeat of a ballot measure constitutes an independent expenditure committee under Government Code Section 82013(b). Congressional newsletters are not included among the list of exempt entities in Government Code Section 86300(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(4).

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that newsletters prepared by Congressmen advocating on ballot measures may constitute reportable expenditures requiring campaign statements unless they meet specific exemptions, with the $500 annual threshold determining committee status.
